defeasance.definition: The action of rendering something null and void; an  instrument that negates or nullifies some other instrument, such as  a will or deed.

defeasible.definition: Able to be revoked, nullified, or undone; usually  applied to estates and land interests that are subject to a condi-  tional limitation and are not absolute.

defect.definition: An imperfection or a flaw; a lack or deficiency.

defect, design.definition: A defect inherent in the design of a product, so  that even when it is correctly made and used it poses a danger to  consumers.

defect, fatal.definition: A defect serious enough to void a contract.

defect, latent.definition: A hidden defect; a flaw that is not apparent to  reasonable observation.

defect, patent.definition: A defect that is immediately obvious upon  normal observation.

defective.definition: Flawed or insufficient; lacking something necessary  to completion.

defective pleading.definition: A pleading that is insufficient or inaccurate  in substance or form.

defective title.definition: Unmarketable title; a title obtained by fraud or  not in fact entirely owned by the purported owner.

defend.definition: To resist an attack; to shield or repel; to represent a  defendant in a lawsuit. See also defendant, defense.

defendant.definition: The party against whom a lawsuit is brought; in civil  cases, the party who responds to the complaint; in criminal cases,  the person against whom charges are brought.

defense.definition: A response, reason, or allegation offered by a defen-  dant to a lawsuit as to why the plaintiff has not established a claim  and should not receive relief; a denial of the plaintiff’s claims or an  attack on the validity of the plaintiff’s causes of action. See also  affirmative defense.

defense attorney.definition: An attorney who normally represents defen-  dants in lawsuits.

defense firm.definition: A law firm that specializes in representing defen-  dants in lawsuits, often paid by the hour. See also insurance  defense, plaintiff’s firm.

defer.definition: To put off to a later time; to postpone. N. deferment,  deferral.

deferral of taxes.definition: Postponing the payment of taxes to a later  year.

deferred compensation.definition: Compensation that is taxed when it is  received as opposed to when it is earned, such as contributions  made by an employer to an employee’s retirement plan that are not  taxed until the retirement funds are distributed.

deficiency.definition: A lack; a shortcoming; in taxation, the amount of tax  owed that has not been paid on time.

deficiency judgment.definition: A judgment issued against someone who  holds a mortgage, imposing personal liability on him or her if a  foreclosure sale does not yield enough money to cover the mort-  gage debt.

deficit.definition: A shortfall; an insufficiency; the condition brought about  by spending more money than is earned; in accounting, the oppo-  site of surplus.

defraud.definition: To take money or property from someone through the  use of fraud or deceit; to cheat someone; to misrepresent a fact  intending for someone to rely on it and thereby harm him or her.  See also fraud.

degree.definition: (1) The amount, level, or extent of something. (2) A  certificate awarded to those who finish a course of study at a  university or college.

degree of crime.definition: A classification of crime based on the level of  guilt involved in committing it.

degree of negligence.definition: A measurement of different kinds of  negligence, used to determine whether someone is sufficiently  negligent to be liable for an injury.

degree of proof.definition: The level of proof needed to convince a court  to render a verdict for a particular case; see also preponderance of  the evidence, clear and convincing, reasonable doubt.

de jure.definition: (Latin) By law, by right; the condition of being in  compliance with all applicable laws; legitimate and lawful. See also  de facto.

delegable duty.definition: A duty that can be performed by someone other  than the one originally intended to do it.

delegate.definition: To entrust someone with a task or responsibility; to  transfer one’s authority to another person. N. A person appointed to  act for someone else; a person elected or appointed to represent  others at a conference or assembly. ADJ. delegable.

delegation.definition: (1) A group of representatives or delegates. (2) The  transfer of power by one branch of the government to another.

deliberate.definition: To consider; to ponder; to examine reasons for and  against something in order to come to a decision. ADJ. Slow and  careful; done with full awareness and intention.

deliberate speed.definition: Immediately; as quickly as practical consider-  ations of law, order, and the welfare of the people will allow; often  used to describe the speed expected by those enacting state-  mandated school desegregation.

deliberation.definition: Careful consideration of a matter, weighing all  arguments and evidence.

delict.definition: A violation of the law; a tort, injury, or crime. (Latin)  delictum.

delicto, ex.definition: (Latin) Arising out of a tort. ADV. ex delicto.

delinquency.definition: Neglect of duty; the commission of minor crimes.

delinquent.definition: (1) Of a person, neglecting one’s duty; failing to  pay a debt; guilty of a crime or failure of duty. (2) Of a debt, due  and unpaid.

delist.definition: To remove something from a list; usually refers to  removing a security from an exchange for failing to meet the  minimum requirements for listing. N. delisting.

deliver.definition: To hand something over to someone else; to voluntarily  transfer title or possession of something to someone else’s posses-  sion or control. N. delivery.

delude.definition: To deceive someone or persuade him or her to believe  something that is false.

delusion.definition: An erroneous belief that is contrary to fact, inappro-  priate to the age, class, and education of the believer, and that  cannot be shaken by evidence to the contrary, usually a result of  some mental disorder; see also insanity. ADJ. deluded.

demand.definition: An insistent request under a claim of right, requiring  someone to do or relinquish something to the demander. V. To  require or insist on something; to ask authoritatively.

demesne.definition: (French) Domain; something owned. N. Ownership of  land held in one’s own right, not subject to the claims of a superior  or occupied by tenants; the phrase “held in demesne” means occu-  pied by the owner as opposed to tenants. ADJ. In pleading, own or  original, i.e., “son assault demesne” means “his own assault.”

demilitarized zone.definition: An area where no military activity is  permitted. ABBRV. DMZ.

de minimis.definition: (Latin) Insignificant or unimportant; not of a level  to interest the law.

de minimis crime.definition: A crime that is not important enough to  justify judicial involvement.

demise.definition: To lease property; to convey property for life or for  years; to bequeath. N. A lease or conveyance of property for a  period of years or for life.

demonstrate.definition: To show; to prove the truth of something by using  logic and evidence; to use practical illustrations to show how  something works or happens. N. demonstration.

demonstrative evidence.definition: Evidence composed of real objects  that appeal directly to the senses, such as maps, charts, illustra-  tions, or the weapon used in committing a crime.

demur.definition: To object to a point of law or fact alleged by the opposing  party on the grounds that it does not advance the interests of the  party making the statement; to present a demurrer.

demurrer.definition: (French) To stop; in common law, a formal allegation  by a defendant that the facts stated in the complaint are true but  nevertheless are not legally sufficient to allow the case to proceed,  and requesting judgment for the defendant and dismissal of the  cause of action; the use of demurrer has mostly disappeared under  the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.

denial, conjunctive.definition: A denial of all allegations as entirely false.

denial, specific.definition: A denial of one specific allegation.

de novo.definition: (Latin) Anew; starting again at the beginning; doing  something a second time as if the first time had not occurred. ADV.  de novo.

de novo trial.definition: A second trial for a matter that has already been  adjudicated; starting over as if the first trial had never happened.

deny.definition: In a pleading, for the defendant to contradict the allega-  tions made in the complaint, to state that the plaintiff’s allegations  are not in fact correct. N. denial.

Department of Homeland Security.definition: A department of the U.S.  Cabinet that is charged with protecting U.S. territory from natural  and man-made hazards, including terrorist attacks. ABBRV. DHS.

Department of Labor.definition: A federal department that dedicates  itself to the U.S. workforce, monitoring working conditions,  regulating wages and retirement benefits, and gathering infor-  mation.

dependency.definition: A territory or possession governed by another  country and geographically separate from the governing country;  e.g., Puerto Rico is a dependency of the United States.

dependent.definition: (1) Needing support from an outside source. (2)  Contingent on some external fact or condition. N. Someone who is  supported by someone else; in taxation, a dependent is usually  someone related to the supporting taxpayer or someone who lives  in the taxpayer’s home.

dependent coverage.definition: Insurance protection for dependents of  the insured.

dependent intervening cause.definition: In common law, something that  happens in between a defendant’s action and its result that occurs  as a normal and predictable response to the defendant’s action;  usually a dependent intervening cause does not break the chain of  causation and the defendant is still held liable for the result of his  or her original action.

dependent promise.definition: A promise in which the promisor does not  have to perform until the other party has done some agreed-upon  duty first.

dependent relative revocation.definition: The principle that if a person  revokes a will intending to replace it with another will, the first will  is actually revoked only if the second will is valid; otherwise, the  first will continue to be in effect.

deplete.definition: To use up a supply or resource; to exhaust assets or  natural resources.

depletion.definition: In taxation, a principle that allows the owner of a  natural resource such as oil or gas to recover the capital cost of the  property as the resource is used up.

depletion allowance.definition: The tax deduction allowed to owners of  property containing natural resources as the resources are  used up.

deponent.definition: A witness who testifies at a deposition.

deport.definition: To send a foreign national from one country to another  because he or she has committed a crime or is of illegal immigra-  tion status. N. deportation. See also extradite.

depose.definition: (1) To question a witness at a deposition. (2) To testify  under oath.

depreciate.definition: To decrease in value over time.

depreciation.definition: The process of spreading the cost of an asset over  a period of years, deducting an allowance for wear and tear as the  asset is used up or worn out.

depreciation, accelerated.definition: A method of depreciation that  provides larger deductions in the earlier years of an asset’s life.

depreciation, reserve.definition: In accounting, an account kept of the  likely depreciation of assets in order to plan for the reduction in  value of assets.

depreciation, straight-line.definition: A method of depreciation in which  the cost of an asset is deducted in equal amounts over its life. See  also accelerated cost recovery system.

depredation.definition: Attacking or plundering; robbing or pillaging.

deputy.definition: An agent; a substitute; someone empowered by a supe-  rior to exercise the functions of the superior’s office and to act on  the superior’s behalf. V. deputize.

deputy sheriff.definition: An officer who has the authority to exercise the  duties of the office of sheriff.

derelict.definition: In poor condition; neglected; abandoned. N. (1) A  homeless person. (2) A boat abandoned on the sea whose master  and crew are not expected to return.

derivative.definition: Coming from, influenced by, or originating in  someone or something else. N. Something that comes from another  person or source. V. derive.

derivative action.definition: (1) A lawsuit brought on behalf of a corpora-  tion by a shareholder. (2) A cause of action founded on an injury to  another person, such as an action for loss of consortium brought  by a wife after her husband is injured.

derivative tort.definition: (1) A civil action in tort based on an injury  caused by criminal conduct by the defendant, in which the plaintiff  seeks compensation for the injury, independent of a criminal action  brought for the same offense. (2) Tort liability imposed on a prin-  cipal for a wrong done by his or her agent.

derogation.definition: The partial repeal or relaxation of a law.

descent.definition: Family origin; heredity; the transmission of property  and goods by inheritance.

descent, collateral.definition: Descent among relatives who share a  common ancestor but are not in a direct line, as between uncle and  nephew or between cousins.

descent, lineal.definition: Descent in a direct line, as from parent to child  or grandparent to grandchild.

descent and distribution.definition: The rules that determine transmission  of a person’s real property if he or she dies intestate, i.e., without a  valid will.

desegregate.definition: To end separation of people by races at school, in  the workplace, or in public facilities. N. desegregation. See also  segregate.

desert.definition: To abandon; to leave willfully with the intention of never  returning. N. desertion.

desist.definition: To stop; to refrain from doing something. See also cease  and desist order.

despotism.definition: A form of government in which one ruler has  absolute power.

destroy.definition: To damage something so badly it can no longer be used  or repaired; to ruin something completely; to demolish or annihi-  late; to nullify or revoke. N. destruction. ADJ. destructive.

desuetude.definition: A condition of disuse; the state of obsolete laws and  practices that are no longer applied because their subject matter no  longer exists, such as laws governing carriages drawn by horses.

detain.definition: To hold in custody; to arrest; to keep or hold back. N.  detention.

detainer.definition: (1) Preventing a person from possessing property or  goods that he or she owns rightfully. (2) A request filed by a crim-  inal justice agency asking a correctional institution to hold a pris-  oner for the agency or to notify the agency when the prisoner is  about to be released.

detainer, unlawful.definition: Wrongfully keeping a person’s property or  goods and refusing to deliver them at the right time or on demand.

determinable.definition: (1) Able to be known or ascertained. (2)  Terminable; able to be ended if a particular condition occurs.

determination.definition: The decision made by a judge, court, or agency  that ends a lawsuit or controversy; a final judgment.

detinue.definition: A common law action brought to recover property that  has been unlawfully taken; see also replevin.

devise.definition: A testamentary gift of real property made in a will. V.  devise; see also bequest, bequeath.

devisee.definition: The person who receives real property through a  devise.

devisor.definition: The person who makes the gift of real property by will.

devolve.definition: To pass from one person to another, often as a result of  the operation of law and without any intentional act by either party;  often used to describe the passing of an estate from a deceased  person to an heir.

dictum.definition: A statement or observation made by a judge about a case  that is not an official part of a judicial opinion, does not embody the  court’s decision, and is not binding. PL. dicta.

dictum, considered.definition: A dictum that is so well-developed it has  gained an air of authority as if it were part of an opinion.

digest.definition: A compilation or summary of a number of books, arti-  cles, cases, or other type of information, often arranged by subject  and indexed for ease of reference; a listing of reported cases  arranged by subject and court.

digital rights management.definition: The practice of restricting access to  digital materials and information by copyright holders and  publishers. ABBRV. DRM.

dilatory.definition: Deliberately slow; intended to delay something.

dilatory plea.definition: A plea by a defendant that does not address the factual merits of the complaint but instead raises procedural or jurisdictional points in an effort to defeat or at least delay the action; very uncommon in modern civil practice.

diligence.definition: Persistence and care; attentiveness; hard work. ADJ.  diligent.

diligence, due.definition: Investigation of the management, operation,  and facts surrounding an investment opportunity; steps that a  person must take to satisfy the requirements in buying or selling  something.

dilute.definition: In corporate law, to lower the value of shares currently  outstanding by issuing new shares. N. dilution.

dilution doctrine.definition: In trademark law, a doctrine prohibiting the  use of a trademark by a second user even if there is no possibility  of confusion so that the uniqueness of the first user’s mark will  be preserved and not weakened by the presence of another use  of the name.

diminished capacity.definition: In criminal law, a mental state that is not  sufficient to have the mens rea needed to commit a crime, brought  on by mental disease or defect, trauma, or intoxication; also called  diminished responsibility.

diminution.definition: Decrease; a reduction in size or number.

diminution in value.definition: A means of measuring damages in a breach  of contract case in which the plaintiff may recover for a decrease  in value to property caused by the breach.

direct attack.definition: An attempt to have a judgment reversed or  vacated by attacking it directly through some process designed to  do that, such as an appeal; see also collateral attack.

directed verdict.definition: Judgment granted for the defendant by a judge  without letting the jury consider the matter in a case where the

direct evidence.definition: Evidence that proves a fact directly, without  inference or presumption; evidence provided by a witness who  actually saw or experienced what happened. See also circum-  stantial evidence, indirect evidence.

director.definition: A person who runs or manages a business, company,  or corporation, often as a member of a board of directors, with a  fiduciary duty to direct its affairs in the best interest of both the  business and its shareholders.

disability.definition: A physical or mental condition that prevents someone  from performing certain acts; lack of legal capacity, caused by a  condition such as infancy or insanity; see also Americans with  Disabilities Act; workers’ compensation.

disability, partial.definition: The condition of being unable to resume one’s  job before injury but being able to perform some suitable gainful  employment.

disability, permanent.definition: Disability that prevents one from ever  returning to one’s former occupation.

disability, total.definition: The condition of being rendered unable to  perform any functions of one’s occupation.

disarmament.definition: The act of destroying, taking away, or getting rid  of weapons.

disaster unemployment assistance.definition: Financial assistance  provided by the U.S. Department of Labor to those whose jobs or  self-employment have been lost or disrupted by a major disaster  declared by the U.S. president.

discharge.definition: (1) To perform a duty; to satisfy a debt. (2) To dismiss  or release someone. (3) To terminate someone’s employment. N.  discharge.

discharge in bankruptcy.definition: The release of a bankrupt person from  his or her debts.

disclaimer.definition: A denial of something, usually one’s own responsi-  bility; a denial of someone else’s claim.

disclaimer, qualified.definition: A written statement made by a taxpayer  refusing to accept an interest in a particular property.

disclose.definition: To reveal; to make known, especially hidden or secret  information. N. disclosure.

discontinuance.definition: An end to a lawsuit brought about voluntarily  by a plaintiff; a nonsuit or dismissal.

discount.definition: An amount subtracted from a larger sum; a deduction  from a loan or advance made by a bank when it buys a negotiable  instrument due at a future date at a price below its face value.

discount rate.definition: (1) A rate used for discounting negotiable instru-  ments purchased by banks in exchange for money or credit. (2)  The minimum interest rate that Federal Reserve banks charge their  members for loans.

discovery.definition: The process of gaining information about an oppo-  nent’s case before trial by reviewing documents, inspecting and  examining property, and asking questions through interrogatories

and depositions.definition: discover. See also Federal Rules of Civil  Procedure.

discredit.definition: To hurt someone’s credibility or reputation; to  make an idea, a statement, an individual, or a piece of evidence  appear unreliable.

discretion.definition: (1) Independent judgment; freedom and authority to  decide how to act; the power given to public officials to act inde-  pendently in fulfilling their duties. (2) The ability to recognize the  difference between right and wrong. (3) Prudence; cautiousness;  circumspection. ADJ. discreet. See also abuse of discretion.

discretion, judicial.definition: A judge’s freedom to make decisions as he  or she sees fit, bound by the principles of law.

discrimination.definition: Unequal or unfair treatment of people based on  categories such as sex, race, religion, or age, when by all rights they  should be treated fairly. V. discriminate. See also civil rights,  Americans with Disabilities Act.

dishonor.definition: (1) To fail to show proper respect. (2) To shame, to  disgrace. (3) To refuse to accept a negotiable instrument such as a  check when presented for payment. N. Shame or disgrace.

disinherit.definition: To take steps such as changing a will to prevent inher-  itance by someone who otherwise would inherit.

disjunctive allegation.definition: A statement in a pleading or a criminal  charge accusing a defendant of two mutually exclusive acts  connected by the word “or,” as in “the defendant murdered or  caused to be murdered.” See also alternative pleading.

dismiss.definition: (1) To allow to leave or to send away. (2) To terminate  someone’s employment. (3) For a judge to refuse to consider a  lawsuit further, thereby ending it before a trial is completed.

dismissal and nonsuit.definition: An end to a case caused by a plaintiff’s  wish to end the matter or a plaintiff’s failure to pursue it.

dismissal with prejudice.definition: A dismissal that prevents the plaintiff  from ever suing on that cause of action again, considered a judg-  ment of the case on its merits and thus res judicata.

dismissal without prejudice.definition: A dismissal that does not preclude  the plaintiff’s attempting to sue on the same cause of action again.

disorder.definition: Confusion, a lack of order; unlawful and riotous  behavior.

disorderly.definition: Contrary to standards of law-abiding, peaceful  behavior.

disorderly conduct.definition: Unruly behavior that disturbs the peace or  shocks public morals.

dispose of.definition: (1) To transfer property to another’s care. (2) To  settle a matter finally.

disposition.definition: (1) Giving up or relinquishing property, especially  through a will. (2) The settlement of a matter; a judge’s final ruling.  (3) In a criminal case, the sentence. (4) Demeanor or personality.

dispossess.definition: To evict or oust from a property, either legally or ille-  gally; to deprive someone of property or land that he or she owns.

dispute.definition: A disagreement or argument; a conflict. V. dispute.

disqualification.definition: The act of disqualifying; the condition that  disqualified someone.

disqualify.definition: To pronounce a person ineligible to perform a  particular task, usually due to some offense or failure to observe  applicable rules.

disseisin.definition: The act of evicting someone from his or her property  and taking it over as one’s own.

dissent.definition: To hold a contrary opinion; to disagree with the  majority or official view of a matter; used especially of judges  presiding over the same case. N. A contrary opinion; an opinion  written by a judge or justice explicitly disagreeing with the  majority decision. ADJ. dissenting.

dissolution.definition: The act of terminating something, such as a busi-  ness, partnership, or contract; the termination of a corporation’s  legal existence; the ending of a marriage.

dissolve.definition: To close, annul, or end; to end the legal existence of a  corporation. See also dissolution.

distinguish.definition: To tell one thing apart from another; to point out  differences between things; to show how one case is significantly  different from another.

distraint.definition: The seizure of a tenant’s personal property by a land-  lord; see also distress. V. distrain.

distress.definition: (1) Anxiety, fear, pain; see also emotional distress. (2)  A landlord’s seizing of personal property without court approval to  satisfy a claim, such as nonpayment of rent; see also distraint. V.  To cause anxiety, pain, or fear.

distribute.definition: To deal out portions or shares of something. N. distri-  bution.

distribution, corporate.definition: A corporation’s paying out money to  shareholders, such as accumulated earnings in the form of  dividends.

distribution, partnership.definition: Payment of a partner by a partnership.

distribution, trust.definition: Payment by a trust to its beneficiaries.

distribution in kind.definition: A distribution of property as it is, instead of  selling it and distributing the proceeds.

distributive.definition: Concerned with the sharing and distribution of  shares of something among recipients.

distributive share.definition: A share that an heir receives from an intes-  tate estate, or that a partner receives from a dissolved partnership.

district.definition: A territory or region of a state or country, defined for  administrative, political, or judicial purposes; a division of a state  under the jurisdiction of a particular federal district court.

district attorney.definition: A public officer of a state or county whose duty  is to prosecute those accused of crimes within the area; different  states use different titles, including prosecuting attorney, county  attorney, state’s attorney, or solicitor. See also United States  Attorney.

district court.definition: (1) A federal court that has jurisdiction over a  particular region of a state and hears cases arising from offenses  against federal laws and cases involving litigants from different

disturb.definition: To interfere with peaceful, normal conditions; to throw  into disorder; to intrude or interrupt.

disturbance.definition: An act that interrupts or annoys someone;  interference with someone’s rights or property; a disruption of  law-abiding, peaceful conditions; a riot.

disturbance of the peace.definition: Conduct that disrupts the quiet and  order of a community by riotous behavior or loud noise.

diversity.definition: Heterogeneity; the state of being composed of different  types of people as opposed to being single-sex, single-race, or  otherwise homogeneous.

diversity of citizenship.definition: The condition that exists when the  parties to a lawsuit come from different states, or when one is a  citizen and one an alien, and that forms the basis of federal diver-  sity jurisdiction.

divestiture.definition: In antitrust law, a remedy in which the court orders  a defendant to sell off assets and property. V. divest.

dividend.definition: Corporate profits and earnings distributed to share-  holders in proportion to the number of shares they own.

dividend, cumulative.definition: A dividend on preferred stock that accu-  mulates if not paid regularly and must be paid before common  stock dividends.

dividend, preferred.definition: A dividend paid on preferred stock.

dividend, stock.definition: A dividend paid in stock instead of money.

dividend addition.definition: A dividend added to the face value of an  insurance policy.

divorce, no-fault.definition: A divorce in which neither spouse must prove  the other guilty of some marital misconduct as grounds for divorce;  also called divorce by consent.

divorce a mensa et thoro.definition: A legal separation in which parties are  forbidden to live together, but that does not end the marriage; also  called divorce from bed and board.

DNA testing.definition: Using evidence contained in DNA to identify or rule  out criminal suspects or fathers in paternity suits.

docket.definition: (1) A list on a court calendar of cases scheduled to be  tried. (2) On appeal, a formal record summarizing the proceedings  of a lower court. V. docket.

document.definition: A piece of written, recorded, printed, or  photographed information or evidence; a physical object or instru-  ment on which information is recorded in letters, numbers,  symbols, or images, including maps, letters, photographs, prints, x-  rays, contracts, deeds, receipts, accounts, etc.

documentary evidence.definition: Evidence consisting of documents  whose authenticity has been established.

document of title.definition: A written document that supplies proof that a  person is entitled to receive and hold particular goods; see also bill  of lading.

document review.definition: A component of discovery in which attorneys  for one party examine documents produced by the other party to  identify the ones relevant to the lawsuit.

doing business.definition: Carrying on or conducting business affairs;  usually used to describe a company that is conducting affairs in a  particular state; see also long arm statute.

doing business as.definition: Phrase used to identify a trade name or the  name of a business. ABBRV. d/b/a.

domain.definition: (1) Total ownership of land; land that is owned  completely. (2) The property owned by a nation or state; synony-  mous with public domain. (3) An area of expertise or influence.

domain name.definition: A name composed of letters and/or numbers,  such as “ebay.com,” or the portion of an email address that follows  the @ sign, linked to a numerical IP address that identifies the loca-  tion of a particular website or computer. See also Domain Name  System, DNS, Uniform Resource Locator, URL.

Domain Name System.definition: The system used to identify information  on the Internet by linking domain names with computer-readable  addresses. ABBRV. DNS.

domestic.definition: (1) Having to do with the home and family relations.  (2) Occurring within a country’s or state’s borders. N. A household  servant.

domestic partner.definition: A person who shares a sexual relationship and  a home with another person but is not necessarily married to him  or her.

domestic violence.definition: Violent physical behavior committed by one  family member against another.

domicile.definition: A legal home; the country or state that a person  considers his or her permanent residence, even if he or she is  living elsewhere; the place where a corporation conducts its  affairs. ADJ. domiciliary.

dominant estate.definition: A piece of property that has the use of an  easement or servitude on another piece of property, usually  adjacent to it.

dominion.definition: Ownership and control over how a piece of property  is used; title and possession.

donation.definition: Something given; a gift. (Latin) donatio. V. donate.

donee.definition: One who receives something from someone else. See  also donor.

donor.definition: One who gives something to someone else; one who  creates a trust, gives a gift, or confers a power. See also donee.

double jeopardy.definition: A second prosecution for an offense after the  defendant has already been tried for it and acquitted; prohibited by  the Fifth Amendment of the Constitution.

doubt.definition: Uncertainty. V. doubt. See also reasonable doubt.

dowager.definition: A widow who holds the title or property that belonged  to her dead husband.

dower.definition: In common law, a widow’s right to a life estate in her  dead husband’s property; no longer exists in most states; see  also curtesy.

downsize.definition: To reduce the size of a workforce in response to  economic conditions or business needs. See also layoff.

dowry.definition: Money and property brought into a marriage by a bride.

draft.definition: (1) A document in which the writer directs someone,  usually a bank, to pay a specific amount of money to a third party;  see also bank draft. (2) A preliminary or preparatory version of a  document. (3) Compulsory induction into the military; see also  conscription, Selective Service System. V. (1) To write a rough or  preliminary version of a document. (2) To select people for  compulsory military service.

draft, sight.definition: A draft of money payable when presented.

draft, time.definition: A draft of money payable on a specific day.

dram shop act.definition: A law that imposes strict liability on a seller of  intoxicating beverages if the sale results in injury to a third party’s  person, property, or means of support due to the intoxication of  the buyer.

drawee.definition: The person or entity on which a draft is drawn, often the  bank.

drawer.definition: The one who writes a draft, i.e., the person who writes a  check from his or her account and signs it.

Dred Scott v. Sandford.definition: An 1857 U.S. Supreme Court decision that ruled that no black person could be a citizen of the United States, that all slaves were the property of their owners and not citizens who could bring lawsuits themselves, and that the government could not revoke a slave owner's right to own slaves based on where he or she lived, invalidating the Missouri Compromise, which had tried to ban slavery in part of the Louisiana territory.

dress code.definition: A set of rules dictating how people should dress in  a given setting, such as students at a school or employees at a  workplace.

driving under the influence.definition: Operating a motor vehicle while  under the influence of alcohol or drugs; also called driving while  intoxicated (DWI) or drunk driving. ABBRV. DUI.

droit.definition: (French) Law; a right; the entire body of law.

drug abuse.definition: Chronic, habitual, uncontrolled, or excessive use of  a controlled substance.

drunkenness.definition: Intoxication; the condition of mental impairment  brought on by consuming too much alcohol.

dual citizenship.definition: The condition of being a citizen of two nations  simultaneously; also the condition of being a citizen of both the  United States and a state.

due.definition: (1) Proper, adequate, rightful, just, appropriate for the  circumstances. (2) Expected to be paid at a particular time;  payable. N. Something owed to someone.

due care.definition: The legal duty a person owes to others in order not  to be negligent; care that is sufficient and proper for the  circumstances. See also care, ordinary.

due process, procedural.definition: The guarantee of a fair civil procedure  that follows the rules, including the right to be notified about a  complaint or charge and the right to be heard in court.

due process, substantive.definition: A guarantee that all legislation that  binds citizens will be fair and reasonable, not arbitrary and unrea-  sonable.

due process of law.definition: Fair judicial treatment guaranteed to every  U.S. citizen by the Constitution in the Fifth and Fourteenth  Amendments, which promise that no citizen will be deprived of  life, liberty, or property without the opportunity to first defend him-  or herself, including the rights to a fair trial, to be present at one’s  trial, to be allowed to present testimony, and to present evidence to  counter the opposing side’s case.

dummy.definition: A sham, imitation, or straw man.

dummy corporation.definition: A corporation with no legitimate business  purpose formed to protect its founders from liability or to hide  their activities.

dummy director.definition: A director of a corporation who is in fact  merely a figurehead and who has no real interest in the corpora-  tion.

dummy shareholder.definition: Someone who holds shares of stock in his  or her own name but for the benefit of the real owner.

dump.definition: To sell goods in quantity at a price far below market value,  often in a foreign market. N. dumping.

duplicity.definition: (1) Double-dealing. (2) In common law, the error of  combining two separate causes of action in one count of a pleading  or a similar mistake in a criminal pleading or legislation; now  permitted under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.

durable power of attorney.definition: A document in which a person  names a surrogate decision maker to consent to or refuse medical

duress.definition: Conduct intended to force someone to do something he  or she does not want to do, such as threat of violence or coercion.

duty.definition: (1) An obligation, often legally sanctioned; in negligence  cases, a legally recognized requirement to observe a specific stan-  dard of conduct toward another person. (2) A tax on imported and  exported goods.

duty free.definition: Not subject to tax on imports or exports; see also  customs.

duty to act.definition: An obligation to perform some act to prevent harm  to someone else.

duty to retreat.definition: The principle that a person being attacked  should always retreat if possible instead of using force in self-  defense; see also castle doctrine.

dwelling.definition: A residence; a house, building, or any enclosed space  or structure used as a home; a house and all buildings attached to  it; also called a dwelling house.

earn.definition: To acquire money in return for work; to perform some task  that makes one deserve some reward.

earnest.definition: Something that one person gives another to mark a  promise or bind a contract, such as a token, pledge, or partial  payment. ADJ. Sincere; truthful.

earnest money.definition: Money paid by a buyer on entering a contract as  proof of his or her intention to pay the full price at the proper time.

earning capacity.definition: The amount of money that a worker is capable  of earning on the open market.

earnings.definition: That which is earned, usually money.

earnings and profits.definition: In tax, income earned by a corporation  that would constitute a dividend if distributed to shareholders.

earnings and profits, accumulated.definition: Earnings and profits from  previous years that have not yet been distributed as dividends.

earnings and profits, current.definition: Earnings and profits from the  current taxable year.

easement.definition: A right to use someone else’s property (the servient  estate or burdened property) for a specific purpose.

easement, affirmative.definition: The right to do some positive act to or on  the servient estate, such as using it as a right of way.

easement, appurtenant.definition: An easement that is attached to a partic-  ular piece of property (the dominant estate or benefited property)  and stays with it if it is transferred to another owner.

easement, implied.definition: An easement that the law will find must have  been intended by the parties to a transaction, even if they did not

easement, negative.definition: An easement that prevents the property  owner from doing specific things on or to the land, usually because  it would impair use of the dominant estate.

easement, prescriptive.definition: An easement that arises in a manner  similar to adverse possession by long use of the servient estate.

easement, public.definition: An easement that the entire community can  use.

easement, reciprocal negative.definition: An easement that automatically  arises when an owner of two or more related properties sells one  of them with a restriction on it, binding the other properties with  the same restrictions.

easement in gross.definition: An easement that belongs to a person, not to  a piece of property.

easement of necessity.definition: An easement that arises by operation of  law if a dominant estate is completely cut off or otherwise  rendered unusable without it.

eavesdrop.definition: To secretly and unlawfully listen to a conversation,  either by entering a private place to listen, installing a listening or  recording device outside a private place, or intercepting commu-  nications over telephones or other devices. N. eavesdropping.  See also wiretapping.

ecclesiastical court.definition: A court with jurisdiction over religious and  church matters.

egalitarian.definition: Supporting equality and fair treatment for all  people. N. egalitarian.

ejectment.definition: A common law action brought by a property owner  to eject a tenant who has refused to leave at the appointed time or  someone claiming the land by adverse possession.

ejusdem generis.definition: (Latin) Of the same kind; a rule of inter-  preting statutes holding that if a statute lists a few examples of  something, then it will be assumed that it only includes things  similar to the examples; e.g., if for “dangerous weapon” it uses  examples of guns, then the term refers only to firearms and not to  other weapons such as knives.

elect.definition: To choose or select; to select a public official; to choose  one legal right from more than one option.

election.definition: (1) The act of choosing. (2) The formal event in which  public officials are chosen. ADJ. elective.

election by spouse.definition: The right of a surviving spouse to choose  how to inherit from the deceased spouse, whether from will,  dower, or his or her share prescribed by statute (the elective  share).

election of remedies.definition: A choice between two or more remedies  for an injury.

elector.definition: (1) Someone who has the right to vote in elections. (2)  A member of the electoral college.

electronic.definition: Done on a computer or over a computer network.

Electronic Communications Privacy Act.definition: A law enacted in 1986  that amended the Wiretap Act to restrict government surveillance  of private electronic communications.

electronic publication.definition: Publishing a work online or on a  computer network.

electronic signature.definition: An electronic symbol, sound, or  process that can be used by a person to verify that he or she is  taking the step of signing a record or document, such as by  keying in a personal identification number or clicking a box to  accept the terms and conditions of a website; also called a  digital signature.

element.definition: A component of some abstract concept; one of the  components of a criminal offense that the prosecution must prove.

elements.definition: Forces of nature such as rain and snow.

eligible.definition: Able or qualified to do or receive something.

email.definition: Electronic mail; correspondence by letter over the  Internet or another computer network.

emancipate.definition: (1) To free someone from the control of someone  else; to liberate. (2) For a parent to relinquish parental rights and  duties toward a child. N. emancipation.

Emancipation Proclamation.definition: A proclamation issued by  Abraham Lincoln in 1862 to free all black slaves in states at war  against the Union.

embezzle.definition: To misappropriate or steal funds entrusted to one’s  care. N. embezzlement.

embracery.definition: The crime of trying to influence a juror with bribes,  promises, etc.; see also obstruction of justice.

emergency doctrine.definition: The principle that a person is not expected  to exercise the same degree of care when faced with an emergency  or sudden peril as he or she would under ordinary circumstances.

eminent domain.definition: The government’s right to take private property  for public use in return for just compensation to the property  owner.

emissions trading.definition: The practice of assigning businesses a certain  number of credits that permit them to emit specified amounts of  pollution, which they can then trade with other businesses that  need to emit more or less pollution than their credits allow; also  called cap and trade.

emolument.definition: The total compensation from a job, including salary,  rank, benefits, and other privileges and advantages.

emotional distress.definition: Severe mental and emotional suffering that  results from another person’s conduct.

employ.definition: To hire someone to do a particular job in exchange for  pay.

employee.definition: One hired to do a job.

employee handbook.definition: A manual given to all employees in a  workplace that describes workplace policies on discipline and  discrimination, defines positions and responsibilities, explains  grievance procedures, and may require employees to sign a form  stating that they have read and accept the terms of the handbook.

employer’s liability acts.definition: Statutes defining employer liability for  injuries that happen to employees while working; see also workers’  compensation.

employment.definition: A job; the act of employing.

Employment Retirement Income Security Ac.definition: An act  of Congress that sets guidelines for employee benefit plans. ABBRV.  ERISA.

enabling clause.definition: A clause in a statute that gives the proper offi-  cials the power to enforce it.

enabling statute.definition: A statute that gives someone a new power.

enact.definition: To make something happen; to create a law.

enacting clause.definition: A clause at the beginning of a statute that iden-  tifies its purpose and the body that has created it.

en banc.definition: (French) On the bench; by the whole court; refers to  occasions when all the judges of a court participate in a session, or  when the panel of judges is expanded for a special case.

encroach.definition: To intrude; to gradually advance into someone else’s  property or territory. N. encroachment.

encryption.definition: The practice of converting information into code  that can only be deciphered with a key.

encumber.definition: To burden; to place a debt or mortgage on a prop-  erty.

encumbrance.definition: A mortgage, debt, or other burden on a piece of  property.

Endangered Species Act.definition: A federal law passed in 1973 that  protects animal species and ecosystems that are threatened with  extinction by human activity.

endow.definition: To bestow something on someone; to provide financial  support by granting property or an income.

endowment.definition: A fund granted to support something, usually an  institution such as a university or charity.

end user license agreement.definition: A contract presented by a software  producer to the product’s end user, i.e., the person who has  acquired the software in order to use it, stating the terms and  conditions associated with using the software. See also browse-  wrap agreement, click-wrap agreement, shrink-wrap agreement.  ABBRV. EULA.

enemy.definition: An adversary; a hostile individual, group, nation, or mili-  tary force.

enfeoff.definition: In feudal times, to give someone property in exchange  for service; in modern usage, to give someone the title to property.  N. enfeoffment.

enforce.definition: To take action to ensure that a law is upheld.

enfranchise.definition: To grant someone the right to vote. N. enfran-  chisement.

enjoin.definition: To order or require someone to do something. See also  injunction.

enjoyment.definition: The ability to use and profit from the property one  owns. See also quiet enjoyment.

enlargement.definition: An extension of time, granted by a court, to do  something that ordinarily must be completed by a specific dead-  line; also called enlargement of time.

enrollment period.definition: A limited period of time in which individuals  can sign up for benefits or health insurance plans, usually for the  upcoming year.

enslave.definition: To make someone a slave. See also indenture,  servitude.

entail.definition: (1) To bring along certain inevitable consequences. (2) To  settle the inheritance of a property within a specific family line over  generations, limiting it to particular descendants; to create a fee  tail. N. An inheritance restricted to a particular family line; property  that is settled in that way.

entailment.definition: A restriction of the way a property will be inherited  that is different from what the ordinary rules of inheritance would  dictate.

enter.definition: (1) To write down in a record; to file or deposit. (2) To go  into a house or building; to go into a country or state. (3) To  become a party to. N. entry.

entitlement.definition: A right granted; a benefit or right guaranteed by law,  such as Social Security.

entity.definition: A being or abstraction, such as a corporation or estate,  that can hold legal rights and obligations, owe and pay taxes, and  sue or be sued.

entrap.definition: To catch someone in a trap; particularly, to induce  someone to commit a crime in order to arrest him or her. N.  entrapment.

enumerated powers.definition: Powers expressly granted by the  Constitution to a branch of government.

en ventre sa mere.definition: (French) In the belly of one’s mother;  unborn but in gestation.

environmental impact statement.definition: A document that a devel-  oper must prepare before receiving approval for a new project,  predicting the project’s likely effect on the surrounding envi-  ronment.

Environmental Protection Agency.definition: A federal agency that coordi-  nates government actions to protect the environment and prevent  pollution. ABBRV. EPA.

equal.definition: The same; having the same value, size, degree, or  quantity.

Equal Employment Opportunity Commission.definition: A federal organiza-  tion created by Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 whose  purpose is to end discrimination in the workplace. ABBRV. EEOC.

equality.definition: The condition of having the same opportunities, rights,  privileges, and liabilities.

equal justice.definition: Equal and impartial treatment of all people by the  law and government.

equal opportunity.definition: A policy of treating employees equally and  not discriminating on the basis of race, sex, religion, national  origin, age, etc.

equal protection (of the laws).definition: The principal that every person  is entitled to the same treatment under the law as other people in  similar conditions.

equal protection clause.definition: A clause in the Fourteenth Amendment  to the Constitution that prohibits states from denying people  within their jurisdictions equal protection of the laws.

Equal Rights Amendment.definition: An amendment to the Constitution  that was proposed but never ratified that would have prevented  the states or nation from denying people equality based on sex.  ABBRV. ERA.

equal time act.definition: An act that requires a broadcaster who allows a  political candidate to campaign on the air to provide equal time on  the air to all other candidates for the office.

equitable.definition: (1) Fair, impartial, just. (2) Arising in equity instead  of law.

equitable distribution.definition: A method of distributing property fairly  but not necessarily equally among interested parties, particularly  between a husband and wife after a divorce.

equitable recoupment.definition: A doctrine that allows a taxpayer who  mistakenly pays taxes too soon and is later assessed taxes for  the same taxable event to reduce the new tax by the amount  already paid.

equity.definition: (1) A branch of jurisprudence that arose in England as an  alternative to the harsh common law in which courts tried to deter-  mine what would be fair in a given situation instead of strictly  applying law and precedent, used in matters where the law was  inadequate; today, equity and the law have merged for the most  part, but equitable principles and remedies still exist. (2) Fairness,  justice. (3) The value of a property minus any mortgages or liens on  it. See also chancery.

equity, court of.definition: A court that hears cases and makes judgments  according to principles of equity; see also chancery.

equity jurisdiction.definition: The body of cases and matters that are  appropriate for hearing in equity.

Erie doctrine.definition: A doctrine developed by the 1938 case Erie v.  Tompkins stating that, except in matters governed by the  Constitution of acts of Congress, in a federal case the court should  apply the state law of the state in which the court is situated  instead of applying federal common law.

erroneous judgment.definition: A judgment rendered using a mistaken  application of the law.

error.definition: A mistake; an inaccurate conception of fact or law.

error, fundamental.definition: An error committed by a trial court that  harms the case enough to render the judgment void.

error, harmless.definition: An error committed by a trial court that  did not harm the rights of the party appealing the judgment  and for which an appellate court will not reverse the trial  court’s judgment.

error, reversible.definition: An error that justifies an appellate court’s  reversing a trial court’s decision. See also assignment of error.

error of fact.definition: A mistake made because an essential fact is not  known or because something that was believed to be a fact was  actually not true; also called error in fact.

error of law.definition: A mistake made in applying the law to a case; also  called error in law.

escalator clause.definition: A clause in a contract that provides for an  increase or decrease in price if a particular event happens.

escape clause.definition: A clause in a contract that allows the parties to  it to break the contract without penalty if a specified event happens  or under specified circumstances.

escheat.definition: The reversion of property to the state if it has no verifi-  able owner or anyone to inherit it. V. escheat.

escrow.definition: A legal document, money, or property entrusted by the  people making a contract to a third person, who holds it until the  contract is finalized and then delivers it to the proper person.

escrow account.definition: A bank account into which funds are held as  security until the escrow condition is fulfilled.

esquire.definition: (1) The title added to the name of an attorney admitted  to the bar. (2) Originally in England, a title used by men who owned  land, or officers, barristers, or judges. ABBRV. Esq.

establishment clause.definition: A clause in the First Amendment of the  Constitution that prevents the government from passing laws  establishing a religion; see also free exercise clause.

estate.definition: (1) An interest in or ownership of land. (2) All the money,  property, and goods that one person owns.

estate, equitable.definition: An estate that can only be enforced in equity,  particularly a trust held in beneficial interest for someone.

estate, future.definition: An estate for which a person might receive title or  interest some time in the future.

estate, legal.definition: An estate recognized and enforceable by law.

estate, vested.definition: An estate that is currently owned or held by the  person to whom the property interest will go when a life estate or  other current interest ends. See also life estate.

estate planning.definition: The branch of law that deals with passing on a  person’s property and assets after death through wills, trusts, and  insurance; also called trusts and estates.

estoppel.definition: A restraint or bar; a doctrine that prevents a person  from doing or saying something that would contradict some  earlier action or statement that another has relied on and the  contradiction of which would hurt that other person. V. estop.  See also collateral estoppel; promissory estoppel.

et al.definition: (Latin) And others; used to indicate the presence of  more people than are named in a category.

et seq.definition: (Latin) And what follows; used in page citations to  instruct the reader to look at the identified page and the following  ones; e.g., “p. 45 et seq.” means “page 45 and following pages.”

European Union.definition: A union of European countries that exists to  create uniform economic, judicial, and security policies throughout  Europe. ABBRV. EU.

euthanasia.definition: Mercy killing; painlessly killing someone who is  suffering from a painful incurable disease or who is in a coma and  has no hope of ever waking up.

euthanasia, active.definition: Actively doing something to kill someone,  such as administering poison.

euthanasia, passive.definition: Withholding artificial support in order to  let a person die naturally; see also right to die, living will.

evade.definition: To avoid, elude, or escape. N. evasion. See also tax  evasion.

evasive.definition: Elusive; trying to escape or avoid.

evasive answer.definition: An answer that avoids a straightforward  response, neither denying nor confirming something.

evict.definition: (1) To recover something from someone through judicial  action. (2) To expel a tenant from property he or she has leased. N.  eviction.

eviction, actual.definition: Physically expelling the tenant from the  property.

eviction, retaliatory.definition: Expelling the tenant from the property in  retaliation for the tenant’s valid complaints about the landlord. See  also constructive eviction.

evidence.definition: Anything used to prove the truth of an issue in court;  includes testimony, documents, objects, and anything else that  could persuade the jury.

evolution.definition: The process by which something grows and changes;  the scientific theory that explains how living organisms have devel-  oped throughout the history of the earth.

examine.definition: To inspect; to investigate; to question or interrogate. N.  examination.

excessive.definition: More than necessary; going beyond what is proper  or fair. N. excess.

excessive bail.definition: Bail set in an amount greater than that required  to prevent the accused from fleeing, prohibited by the Eighth  Amendment of the Constitution.

exchange.definition: To give something to another person and receive  something else in return; to barter. N. (1) The act of giving one thing  and receiving something else in return. (2) An institution that facil-  itates the trading of a commodity such as stocks or bonds. (3) A  transaction in which people living far away from each other settle  accounts by transferring credits or drafts called bills of exchange.

exchequer.definition: The national treasury of the British government.

excise.definition: A tax on certain actions or occupations, the manufacture  and sale of particular items, or the transfer of property; also called  excise tax.

exclude.definition: To deny access or entrance; to keep out; to remove.

exclusion.definition: (1) Refusing admittance or removing. (2) In taxation,  an item that is not included in gross income; see also deduct. (3) In  insurance, something that is not covered by a policy.

exclusionary rule.definition: A rule stating that evidence found during  illegal searches and seizures cannot be used at trial.

exclusive.definition: Applying only to one subject, excluding all others.

exclusive contract.definition: A contract in which a person promises to  buy or sell from one source only.

exclusive control.definition: Control over something exercised by only  one person.

exclusive jurisdiction.definition: Power over a kind of lawsuit or person  that is held by only one court or tribunal, requiring that all actions  of that sort be heard there.

exclusive license.definition: A right granted by the owner of a patent to  one person, allowing him or her to use, make, and sell the  patented item.

exclusive use.definition: In trademark law, the exclusive right to use a  specific mark and other marks similar enough to be confusing.

exculpate.definition: To show that someone is not guilty of a crime or  wrongful act; to clear of blame or fault. ADJ. exculpatory.

exculpatory clause.definition: A clause in a legal document that releases a  party from liability for wrongdoing.

exculpatory evidence.definition: Evidence that helps clear a defendant of  guilt.

excusable neglect.definition: Failure to perform the right action at the  right time due to unexpected and unavoidable circumstances  rather than through carelessness or disregard.

excuse.definition: To forgive; to release from responsibility. N. A reason  given to justify an action. ADJ. excusable.

ex-dividend.definition: Without dividend; the condition of a stock sold  without a recently declared dividend, which is retained by the  seller.

executive.definition: Having the power to make things happen and carry  out laws; the power held by the president of the United States.

executive agreement.definition: An agreement similar to a treaty that the  president of the United States can make with another country  without Senate approval.

executive clemency.definition: A chief executive’s right to pardon crimi-  nals and commute criminal sentences.

executive order.definition: An order issued by the president interpreting  the Constitution or a law.

executive privilege.definition: An executive’s freedom from the disclosure  laws that bind other citizens when secrecy is necessary to fulfill the  duties of the office.

executor.definition: A person chosen by a testator to give away his or her  property according to his or her will. FEMININE. executrix or  executress.

executory.definition: Not yet complete; awaiting a future event for  completion.

executory contract.definition: A contract that will not be complete until  some specific event happens.

executory interest.definition: A future estate or interest in land.

executory trust.definition: A trust that has a settlement or conveyance still  to make in the future.

exemplar.definition: Evidence other than testimony taken from a defen-  dant and used to identify him or her, including fingerprints, voice-  prints, blood samples, handwriting samples, etc.

exemplary damages.definition: Damages awarded on top of actual  damages if the wrong done to the plaintiff by the defendant was  aggravated in some way.

exempt.definition: Not under an obligation that applies to others;  free from liability or obligation. V. To release someone from an  obligation or liability.

exemption.definition: (1) Freedom from a normal obligation. (2) In  taxation, a deduction given to a taxpayer for him- or herself and  for dependents.

exhaustion of remedies.definition: The state of having attempted and  failed to get a remedy through administrative channels before  bringing a matter to litigation, or through state court before  bringing a matter before a federal court.

exhibit.definition: To display something; to present something publicly. N.  A piece of physical evidence or a document displayed in court or  during a deposition or hearing to be made part of the case.

exigent circumstances.definition: An emergency situation in which imme-  diate action is necessary, regardless of procedural requirements;  generally applied to situations in which law enforcement officials  conduct a search and seizure without a warrant.

exile.definition: Banishment from a country; a person who has been  expelled from a country. V. exile.

ex officio.definition: (Latin) Out of duty; by virtue of one’s office or  status; used to describe powers that are not explicitly defined in an  officer’s duties but that are implied because they are necessary to  the performance of the office.

ex officio justice.definition: A judge who serves in a particular capacity  because it is part of his or her job description, not because he or  she was chosen personally to perform that task.

ex officio member.definition: Someone who is a member of a board or  other group due to a title or office he or she holds.

exonerate.definition: To absolve; to release from blame or obligation. N.  exoneration.

ex parte.definition: (Latin) From a side; on behalf of one side of a case.

ex parte divorce.definition: A divorce proceeding in which only one  spouse attends or participates.

ex parte hearing.definition: A judicial hearing held for the benefit of one  party only, without notice to or attendance by an adverse party.

ex parte injunction.definition: An injunction issued by a court after hearing  only the party requesting it.

expectancy.definition: Something that is expected or at least hoped for; in  property, an interest or estate that is expected to occur when some  specific event happens in the future. See also life expectancy.

expectant.definition: Hoping to receive something in the future.

expel.definition: To force out; to eject; see also expulsion.

expense.definition: Cost; the amount of money spent on something.

expense, business.definition: An expense that is part of running a business.

expense, personal.definition: Money spent on personal and family needs.

expenses.definition: Costs incurred in performing a job; money that must  be spent in the ordinary course of business or daily operations.

expenses, operating.definition: The ordinary and regular costs involved in  operating a business.

expert.definition: Someone with great knowledge about a particular topic,  such as science, medicine, art, or business. See also lay witness.

expert testimony.definition: Opinions about a subject offered by an expert  in the field in order to help the jury understand specialized  evidence in a case.

expert witness.definition: A witness who has training or experience in a  field that gives him or her knowledge about it far beyond that of an  ordinary person, whose testimony is provided to help the jury  understand difficult technical information.

expiry.definition: The end of something, such as a contract period or a  period of time.

export.definition: To transport goods from one country to another to sell  them. N. Goods made in one country and sold in another. See  also import.

ex post facto.definition: (Latin) From the point of view of subsequent  events; after the fact; retroactive.

ex post facto law.definition: A law that is passed after an occurrence that  changes the law at the time the act occurred; e.g., a law that creates

express.definition: Clear and explicit; directly stated; unambiguous. V. To  state definitely.

expropriate.definition: To take for the government under eminent domain.  N. expropriation.

expulsion.definition: Driving out; ejecting from a group, school, etc;  depriving of membership; see also expel.

expunge.definition: To erase; to destroy completely, especially information  or documents.

expungement of records.definition: Court-ordered destruction of criminal  records after a certain period of time or if an accused is not  convicted.

expurgate.definition: To cleanse; to remove objectionable material from a  document.

ex rights.definition: Without rights; the condition of a stock sold without  rights to purchase more stock from the issuing corporation.

extend.definition: To make larger; to prolong; to push back the original  boundary or deadline.

extension.definition: (1) An allowance of additional time after a deadline  has expired. (2) An addition that makes something bigger, such as  a structure added to a building to make it larger.

extenuate.definition: To mitigate; to make a crime or misdeed seem less  wrong.

extenuating circumstances.definition: Circumstances that make a crime  appear less serious and make the one who committed it appear less  blameworthy than would ordinarily be the case.

extinguish.definition: To end; to cancel; to void a right; to quench or put  out, as a fire.

extinguishment.definition: The end of a right or contract by agreement  between the parties or by operation of law.

extort.definition: To get something from someone else through force,  threats, or fear. N. extortion.

extortionate.definition: (1) Far more expensive than is appropriate. (2)  Prone to extortion.

extradite.definition: For a state or country to transfer a person accused of  a crime to the country or state where the crime supposedly  occurred. N. extradition.

extrajudicial.definition: Done outside of court; not legally authorized.

extramural.definition: Outside the walls, boundaries, or limits of an insti-  tution or business; in corporations, outside the limits of corporate  power. See also intramural.

extraordinary circumstances.definition: Circumstances and factors not  usually associated with a particular time, place, or situation.

extraordinary risk.definition: A risk not normally associated with a partic-  ular activity.

eyewitness.definition: Someone who sees an event firsthand.

fabricate.definition: To invent; to create; to make up a story or manufacture  an object, usually intending to deceive. N. fabrication.

fabricated evidence.definition: Evidence created after the fact to disguise  the truth.

face value.definition: The value of a check, bond, note, insurance policy,  or other negotiable instrument as stated on the instrument itself;  see also market price.

facilitate.definition: To assist; to make something possible or easier to  accomplish; to make it easier for someone to commit a crime. N.  facilitation. See also aid and abet.

facility.definition: A building, a space, or the equipment used to  provide some service or perform some function; an amenity.  PL. facilities.

facsimile.definition: An exact copy of something, usually a document.

fact.definition: Something that is true; a thing that has happened or a  situation that exists.

fact finder.definition: The person or group of people whose job is to  determine the facts in a case; also called the trier of fact. See also  jury.

factor.definition: (1) Something that influences an outcome. (2) An agent  who buys and sells goods for a commission called a factorage.

factual.definition: Truthful; describing actual events or circumstances.

faculties.definition: Physical and mental capabilities; the ability to do  something.

failure of consideration.definition: A condition that occurs when a  contract’s consideration becomes worthless or no longer exists.

failure of issue.definition: The condition of producing no children to  inherit one’s estate; dying childless.

failure of justice.definition: Miscarriage of justice; a loss of rights or lack  of reparation due to the absence of an adequate legal remedy.

failure of proof.definition: Inability to prove a side of a case.

failure to state a claim (upon which rel.definition: A  plaintiff’s failure to define and support a cause of action sufficient  to support a case in court, resulting in dismissal of the case; see  also Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.

fair.definition: Impartial and just; according to stated rules and  standards.

fair and impartial jury.definition: A jury composed of people who have no  preconceptions about a case and who will form opinions based on  a fair consideration of evidence and testimony.

fair and impartial trial.definition: A trial or hearing before a court or jury  that has no preconceived notions about the matter and that will  hear testimony and examine evidence before making a decision.

fair comment.definition: A defense in libel cases, used especially by the  news media, that the published statements in question were meant  to state facts that the writer or speaker believed were true.

fair hearing.definition: An administrative hearing authorized in a case  where a normal judicial proceeding would not satisfy the  requirements of due process, such as a case in which no judicial

Fair Housing Act.definition: A federal law passed as Title VIII of the Civil  Rights Act of 1968 that prohibits discrimination in the rental, sale,  and financing of housing based on race, sex, religion, national  origin, handicap, and family status.

Fair Labor Standards Act.definition: A federal statute passed in 1938 that  set a minimum wage, a maximum work week, and established  rules about work by teenagers for businesses engaged in interstate  commerce.

fair use.definition: The legal use of copyrighted materials without the  owner’s consent or payment of royalties; whether a use is a fair use  or an infringement of copyright depends on factors such as who is  using the material, the amount used, and whether or not the user  acknowledges the copyright owner; see also copyright, plagiarize.

faith-based initiative.definition: A charitable project undertaken by a  religious organization.

false advertising.definition: The deliberate inclusion of false information  in advertising, with the intention of misleading consumers in order  to make them purchase the advertised product. See also advertise.

false arrest.definition: Unlawfully arresting or detaining someone without  legal authority or the detainee’s consent.

false imprisonment.definition: The tort of detaining someone intentionally  and without justification.

false representation.definition: Intentionally misleading someone either  by lying or by failing to disclose a fact when the fact should have  been disclosed.

false statement.definition: A statement made by someone who knows it is  false or who makes it recklessly without honestly believing it to be  true, intending it to deceive.

false swearing.definition: When a person swears that something is true  and he or she knows that it is not; also called a false oath.

family.definition: A group of people related to one another by blood and  marriage, often sharing a common ancestor; the unit consisting of  parents and children; for insurance purposes, the people who live  with and are dependent on an insured person.

family allowance.definition: Money allocated to a surviving spouse and/or  children to support them while the deceased spouse’s estate goes  through the probate process; see also widow’s allowance.

Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993.definition: A federal law that  protects the jobs of and guarantees unpaid leave to certain workers  who suffer medical problems or who must care for sick family  members or new children. ABBRV. FMLA.

family automobile doctrine.definition: A doctrine, also called a family  purpose doctrine, that makes the owner of an automobile liable for  negligent operation of it by a member of his or her family; this  doctrine has been rejected by many states.

family court.definition: A court with jurisdiction over matters related to  families and children, including child abuse and neglect, support  and custody, paternity, and juvenile delinquency.

fatal.definition: Deadly; causing death; causing something to fail  completely.

fatal error.definition: An error at trial that hurts one party’s case so badly  as to be grounds for a new trial.

fatal injury.definition: An injury that causes death to its victim.

fault.definition: Blame; responsibility for a bad result; a wrongful act or  mistake; negligence; breach of duty.

fealty.definition: Sworn loyalty; in feudal times, allegiance to a feudal lord  sworn by a tenant.

fear, uncertainty, and doubt.definition: A marketing strategy in which the  manufacturer of a product tries to make consumers worry that  buying a competing product might result in bad consequences,  typically by criticizing or spreading disinformation about the  competing product. ABBRV. FUD.

featherbedding.definition: A practice by employees of increasing or main-  taining the number of workers required to perform a task or the  time allotted to do a specific job in an effort to protect job security,  especially against the threat of technology.

Federal Aviation Administration.definition: The federal agency that  handles civil aviation as part of the Department of Transportation.  ABBRV. FAA.

Federal Bureau of Investigation.definition: The federal agency that inves-  tigates violations of federal laws, including criminal, civil, and  security matters as part of the Department of Justice. ABBRV. FBI.

federal common law.definition: A body of case law composed of decisions  made by federal courts, used in a very narrow range of cases; see  also Erie doctrine.

Federal Communications Commission.definition: The federal agency that  regulates broadcasting by television, radio, telephone, satellite, etc.  ABBRV. FCC.

federal courts.definition: The courts of the United States, created by the  Constitution or by acts of Congress and having jurisdiction created  by statute, including federal district courts, federal courts of  appeal, and the Supreme Court of the United States.

Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation.definition: An agency within the  executive branch of the U.S. government that insures deposits in  banks and savings associations. ABBRV. FDIC.

Federal Emergency Management Agency.definition: An agency of the U.S.  Department of Homeland Security dedicated to coordinating  national responses to major disasters such as hurricanes or earth-  quakes. ABBRV. FEMA.

Federal Firearms License.definition: A license issued by the Bureau of  Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives that allows an indi-  vidual or business to manufacture or sell firearms and ammuni-  tion. ABBRV. FFL.

Federal Insurance Contribution Act.definition: The act that regulates the  Social Security system by taxing employers and employees and  setting benefits. ABBRV. FICA.

federalism.definition: The federal system of government, including a divi-  sion of power between a central government and individual states,  in which states handle local affairs and the central government  handles matters that affect the entire nation.

Federalist Papers.definition: A collection of essays published by  Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay from October  1787 to August 1788, advocating that New York vote to ratify the  Constitution that had already been adopted in Philadelphia in  September 1787.

federal question jurisdiction.definition: Original jurisdiction held by  federal courts over cases in which interpretation of the  Constitution, acts of Congress, or treaties of the United States are  in dispute; federal question jurisdiction is granted to federal courts  by Article III of the Constitution.

Federal Reserve System.definition: A network of twelve central banks  established by the Federal Reserve Act in 1913 to oversee a  national system of banking, including monitoring the money  supply by holding cash reserves and issuing currency for circu-  lation, overseeing the credit system, and generally watching the  nation’s economy.

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.definition: A compilation of rules created  by the Supreme Court in 1938 that govern the procedure used in  civil cases in U.S. district courts, which have been used as a model  for procedure by most state courts.

Federal Rules of Evidence.definition: Rules governing the admission of  evidence before U.S. district courts, U.S. magistrates, and  bankruptcy court, which have been used as a model for rules  of evidence by many states.
